core_concept: Body Systems in Detail: Digestive, Circulatory, and Respiratory Systems
facts: The digestive system breaks food into nutrients; the mouth chews and adds saliva to start digestion, not just to swallow.
The heart pumps blood through arteries to deliver oxygen to the body, while veins return blood to the heart, not the other way around.
The small intestine is where most nutrient absorption happens, not the stomach, because the stomach's main job is to break down food with acid.
Air moves from the nose through the trachea to the lungs, where oxygen enters the blood in the alveoli, not in the throat or windpipe.
The circulatory system carries oxygen from the lungs to the body's cells, but the respiratory system itself only moves air in and out of the lungs.
misconceptions: Students think the stomach is where nutrients are absorbed into the blood, but the small intestine is the main site of absorption.
Students believe the heart pumps blood only to the lungs, not realizing it pumps oxygen-rich blood to the whole body through arteries.
